skill.name: agent-browser
skill.description: Automates browser sessions through inference.sh using Playwright-style actions, compact element refs, screenshots, video recording, proxies, file uploads, and page JavaScript execution.
skill.summary: Control browser sessions for web automation, capture, testing, and research workflows.
skill.author: inference-sh-0

security_audit.summary: Static analysis found many shell, network, filesystem, and credential-related patterns. Review shows most shell and URL matches are documentation examples or user-run templates, but the skill intentionally enables powerful browser automation with authenticated sessions, screenshots, video, proxies, file upload, and page JavaScript execution. No prompt injection or confirmed malicious exfiltration was found, so this is publishable only with clear warnings about sensitive browsing and credential use.

medium_findings.title: Powerful Remote Browser Automation
medium_findings.description: The skill grants an AI workflow the ability to open arbitrary URLs, click controls, fill fields, upload files, take screenshots, and execute page JavaScript through the inference.sh browser service. This is expected for the skill, but it can affect real accounts and sensitive web pages if used without user review.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The advertised capabilities and command reference directly confirm broad browser control. The behavior is disclosed, so this is dual-use risk rather than hidden malicious behavior.

medium_findings.title: Credential and Cookie Handling Guidance
medium_findings.description: The authentication documentation shows login automation using password variables and a cookie extraction workflow using document.cookie. These examples can support legitimate testing, but they can expose session data when used on sensitive accounts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The file explicitly describes credential filling and cookie extraction. The surrounding documentation also includes safety advice, which lowers confidence of malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Proxy Rotation and Anonymity Support
medium_findings.description: The proxy guide includes authenticated proxies, SOCKS5 proxy use, privacy routing, and a rate-limit avoidance example. Proxy support is legitimate for geo-testing and corporate networks, but rate-limit avoidance is a dual-use scraping pattern.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The documentation directly names rate-limit avoidance and SOCKS proxy routing. Legitimate examples are also present, so the risk is contextual.

medium_findings.title: Screenshots, Video, and Local Capture Outputs
medium_findings.description: The skill can capture screenshots and full session video, and templates save page text, links, screenshots, and video metadata to local files. This can leak sensitive page contents when used on authenticated or private sites.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The capture and recording behavior is clearly documented. The video reference warns against sensitive sessions, reducing concern about malicious intent.

medium_findings.title: Local File Upload Capability
medium_findings.description: The interact action accepts file_paths and the main guide shows uploading a local file path to a web form. This is useful for test automation, but it can disclose local files if a user authorizes unsafe prompts.
medium_findings.confidence_reasoning: The upload action and file_paths field are explicit. The evidence does not show automatic file selection or stealth upload.

low_findings.title: Shell Pattern Flood Is Mostly Documentation and Templates
low_findings.description: The many shell command and command substitution alerts primarily come from bash examples and reusable scripts that invoke infsh and jq. The skill metadata restricts allowed Bash usage to infsh commands, but the templates still require normal shell caution when copied and run.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The matched patterns are visible shell examples, not hidden runtime code in an application package. Some risk remains because users may execute the templates locally.

low_findings.title: Hardcoded URL Matches Are Mostly Examples
low_findings.description: Hardcoded URL alerts point to inference.sh documentation, example.com, Google, httpbin, and proxy placeholders. I found no evidence that the skill silently sends collected data to an unrelated endpoint.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The URLs are examples or the declared inference.sh service. No suspicious destination or concealed network exfiltration was found in the reviewed files.

low_findings.title: Weak Cryptography Alerts Appear to Be False Positives
low_findings.description: The static weak-crypto blocker hits align with markdown tables, password field names, and example content rather than cryptographic functions. No evidence found of MD5, SHA1, custom encryption, or password hashing logic in the reviewed skill files.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The cited locations contain documentation structure and field names, not crypto implementation. Confidence is not 1.0 because the static list is broad, but sampled evidence supports dismissal.

dangerous_patterns.title: Pipe to Shell Installation Command
dangerous_patterns.description: The quick start recommends piping a downloaded installer script directly to sh. This is a real supply-chain risk if the download endpoint or transport is compromised, even though it is a common CLI installation pattern.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The exact pipe-to-shell pattern is present in the quick start. It is user-visible setup guidance, not stealth execution by the skill.

dangerous_patterns.title: Arbitrary JavaScript Execution in Page Context
dangerous_patterns.description: The execute function accepts a code string and runs JavaScript in the current page context. This can read page content and interact with the DOM, so it requires trusted prompts and trusted target sites.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The command reference directly documents a code field for JavaScript execution. The risk is inherent to the advertised feature.

dangerous_patterns.title: Cookie Extraction Example
dangerous_patterns.description: The authentication guide shows extracting document.cookie and printing the result. This can disclose session cookies when used on authenticated pages.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The cookie extraction command and output variable are explicit. Browser protections may limit httpOnly cookies, but readable cookies remain sensitive.

dangerous_patterns.title: Proxy Rotation for Rate-Limit Avoidance
dangerous_patterns.description: The proxy guide includes a rate-limit avoidance section that rotates proxies across URLs. This can support abusive scraping if used against sites without permission.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The section explicitly names rate-limit avoidance and shows proxy rotation. The same proxy feature also has legitimate uses.

dangerous_patterns.title: Local File Path Uploads
dangerous_patterns.description: The upload action accepts local file paths and sends selected files through the browser session. Users must restrict uploads to intended test files.
dangerous_patterns.confidence_reasoning: The file_paths capability is documented, but there is no evidence of automatic discovery or unauthorized file selection.

content.value_statement: Manual web workflows are slow to repeat and hard to verify. This skill lets Claude, Codex, and Claude Code drive browser sessions, capture evidence, and extract page data.
actual_capabilities: Open web pages through inference.sh browser sessions.
Interact with page elements using compact @e references.
Fill forms, click buttons, select options, scroll, drag, and upload files.
Take viewport or full-page screenshots and record session video.
Run JavaScript in the active page to extract text, links, and DOM data.
Route sessions through HTTP, HTTPS, or SOCKS proxy settings.
limitations: Requires the inference.sh CLI and an authenticated inference.sh account.
Element refs become stale after navigation or dynamic page changes.
Browser actions can expose credentials, cookies, screenshots, or uploaded files.
Iframe, CAPTCHA, and complex anti-bot flows may need manual handling.

use_cases.title: QA Browser Checks
use_cases.description: Run repeatable browser actions, capture screenshots, and record failures for review.

use_cases.title: Research Page Capture
use_cases.description: Open public pages, collect visible text and links, and save evidence screenshots.

use_cases.title: Admin Workflow Automation
use_cases.description: Automate approved internal web tasks after confirming element refs and session state.

prompt_templates.title: Open and Summarize a Page
prompt_templates.prompt: Open this URL with agent-browser, list the visible interactive elements, and summarize the page purpose: <url>

prompt_templates.title: Capture Evidence
prompt_templates.prompt: Open <url>, take a full-page screenshot, capture the page title and current URL, then close the browser session.

prompt_templates.title: Test a Form Safely
prompt_templates.prompt: Use agent-browser to inspect the form at <url>. Show the refs first, wait for my approval, then fill only the test values I provide.

prompt_templates.title: Extract Structured Page Data
prompt_templates.prompt: Open <url>, use the latest snapshot, run JavaScript only to read visible page data, extract headings and links, save screenshots, and close the session.

output_examples.input: Open a product page and capture the visible buying options.
output_examples.output: The browser session opened the page, identified product option controls, captured a screenshot, and summarized the visible price and availability.

output_examples.input: Check whether the contact form shows a success message after test submission.
output_examples.output: The workflow inspected the form refs, filled approved test values, submitted the form, and reported the final page title, URL, and success text.

output_examples.input: Record a short walkthrough of the settings page.
output_examples.output: The session recorded the approved navigation steps, saved a video reference after closing, and noted that sensitive fields were not recorded.

best_practices: Use test accounts and test data for form and login workflows.
Review element refs before submitting forms, uploading files, or changing account settings.
Disable video and screenshots when pages contain credentials or private data.
anti_patterns: Do not run login or cookie extraction workflows on accounts you do not control.
Do not use proxy rotation to bypass site rules, rate limits, or access controls.
Do not upload local files unless the file path and destination are explicitly approved.

faq.question: Does this skill browse the web directly?
faq.answer: It controls browser sessions through the inference.sh CLI and agent-browser app.

faq.question: Can it interact with authenticated pages?
faq.answer: Yes, but use test accounts when possible and avoid recording sensitive sessions.

faq.question: Why do element refs change?
faq.answer: Refs are generated from the current page snapshot and can change after navigation or DOM updates.

faq.question: Can it upload files?
faq.answer: Yes. It supports file upload inputs when you provide explicit local file paths.

faq.question: Does it support proxies?
faq.answer: Yes. It documents HTTP, HTTPS, authenticated, and SOCKS proxy options.

faq.question: Is it safe for sensitive sites?
faq.answer: Use caution. Screenshots, video, cookies, page text, and uploaded files may contain sensitive data.
